title: Freezing Foods
content:
FREEZING

GENERAL PRINCIPLES

Quick freezing at 0°F or below prevents formation of large ice crystals that damage cell structure. Maintain constant low temperature—fluctuations cause recrystallization and texture damage. Air in package causes freezer burn—exclude all air.

EQUIPMENT

Use moisture-vapor-proof materials: freezer paper, heavy-duty aluminum foil, freezer bags, rigid containers with tight lids. Label all packages with contents and date.

VEGETABLES

Most require blanching before freezing to stop enzyme action that causes off-flavors, loss of color, texture changes.

Blanching: Small quantities in rapidly boiling water (1 gallon water per pound vegetables). Time according to vegetable size/density (1-5 minutes usually). Plunge immediately into ice water (same duration as blanching). Drain thoroughly; package immediately; exclude air; seal; freeze at once.

Vegetables best for freezing: Beans, peas, corn, broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, spinach, asparagus.

Exceptions (do not freeze well): Salad greens, raw tomatoes, raw potatoes, celery, cucumbers, radishes.

FRUITS

Select firm, ripe, perfect fruit. Wash; prepare as for eating (pare, pit, slice). Some fruits darken when cut—dip in ascorbic acid solution or sugar syrup.

Dry pack: Arrange in single layer on tray; freeze until firm; package; return to freezer. Good for berries to use individually.

Syrup pack: Cover with 30-50% sugar syrup; leave ½ inch headspace; seal; freeze.

Sugar pack: Sprinkle with sugar; let stand until sugar dissolves and fruit makes own syrup; package; freeze. Good for soft fruits.

Unsweetened pack: Cover with water or unsweetened juice; leave ½ inch headspace; seal; freeze.

Fruits best for freezing: Berries, cherries, peaches, apricots, plums, apples, rhubarb, citrus.
